exemplar.title: To what extent do the talent management strategies employed by the Taj Group influence its organisational performance?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do the talent management strategies employed by the Taj Group influence its organisational performance?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and comprehensively, with clear definitions of key terms (âtalent management,â âTajness,â âorganisational performanceâ).
Analytical sections consistently link back to the research focus, demonstrating effective communication of how HR practices shape outcomes.
The research question is prominently stated and maintained throughout, giving the study a coherent frame.
weaknesses: The research question remains quite broad and lacks specific performance metrics or a defined time frame to sharpen focus.
The methodology description omits explicit criteria for source credibility and selection rationale.
Primary research is absent and limitations of secondary data are only implied later in the essay.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of the topic is exemplary and key terms are defined in context, meriting the top of the 6-mark band. However, the research question is only partially focused and the methodology lacks clear evidence of informed selection, preventing a perfect score. A 5/6 reflects the strong, consistent explanation balanced against those identifiable weaknesses.

strengths: Source material is applied clearly and appropriately, with industry statistics and annual-report data integrated to support key arguments.
Theoretical frameworks (Herzberg, Maslow, Porterâs Five Forces) are accurately identified and aligned with the research objectives.
Terminology is used accurately and consistently in context, demonstrating a strong command of subject-specific concepts.
weaknesses: A few claims rely on generic statements without direct evidence, leading to occasional loose links between cited data and analysis.
Citation formatting is inconsistent, though this is more a presentation issue than knowledge base.
Some applications of theory (e.g., Maslow mapping) remain surface-level rather than fully exploiting source depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay consistently demonstrates clear, coherent subject knowledge and applies relevant source material appropriately. Occasional reliance on generic statements and minor lapses in integration keep it from the very top of the 6-mark band, but the overall command of concepts and sources justifies a 5/6.

strengths: The research draws on a broad array of secondary evidence (annual reports, HBR cases, labour statistics), all clearly pertinent to the research question.
Analysis is well structured, with cause-and-effect links (e.g., recruitment strategies lowering bargaining power) supported by quantitative figures.
The argument is balanced and reasoned, weighing both successes (low turnover, strong brand) and challenges (cost pressures, workâlife balance).
weaknesses: No primary data are included, and some secondary sources (e.g., blogs) vary in academic rigor.
Evaluation of counter-arguments is brief and there is little explicit critical appraisal of data limitations.
Quantitative depth (ROI calculations, trend analyses) is limited, reducing the sophistication of the evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality, analysis depth and evaluation consistently fall into the strong mid-band (4â6) performance: evidence is relevant and wellâintegrated, and arguments are reasoned but not yet fully sophisticated. Awarding the top of that band (6/12) reflects solid critical thinking while acknowledging the remaining gaps in depth and critical evaluation.

strengths: All required structural elements (title page, contents, headings, bibliography) are present and logically ordered.
Sections flow coherently under clear headings and subheadings, facilitating navigation.
weaknesses: Citation style is inconsistent, with mixed bracketed and formal references.
Table of contents leader dots vary in length, disrupting visual alignment.
Occasional typographical errors and broken URL line breaks compromise polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report meets the core structural requirements and presents a clear layout, satisfying the mid-band structure criterion. However, multiple formatting inconsistencies and typographical lapses indicate that layout considerations are not fully and correctly applied. A 3/4 appropriately captures this level of presentation quality.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is prominently stated on the title page and repeated in the rationale, giving the inquiry a coherent frame.  However, the wording (âTo what extent do the talent management strategies â¦ influence organisational performance?â) remains quite broad; neither specific performance metrics nor a defined time frame are embedded, and the subsequent discussion sometimes drifts into general culture-building rather than measurable influence.  As a result, the question is clearly stated and mostly maintained, yet only partially focused.

The student builds a balanced discussion that recognises both successes (low turnover, strong brand) and problems (work-life balance, rising costs).  A reasoned conclusion addresses the research question and weighs evidence.  However, evaluation of counter-arguments is brief, and criteria for judging âextent of influenceâ are implicit rather than explicit, limiting critical depth.  Thus the argument is well-developed but only partially critical.

The discussion demonstrates sound understanding of HR conceptsâHerzberg, Maslow, Balanced Scorecard and Porterâs Five Forces are explained correctly and applied to Tajâs context.  Yet some analysis remains surface-level (e.g., the Maslow mapping stops at description) and a few assertions about cost pressures or cultural alignment lack empirical depth.  Knowledge is clear and coherent, but source use to develop that knowledge is only partially effective.

Business-management terminologyââintrinsic motivationâ, âjob enrichmentâ, âbargaining powerâ, âmoments of truthâ etc.âis generally accurate and used in the correct context.  Occasional slips (e.g., âcentrismâ for âcentricityâ, a few grammatical lapses) and inconsistent citation formatting prevent the usage from being fully polished and entirely consistent, but overall command of concepts is mostly accurate.

Cause-and-effect links between HR practices and performance (e.g., rural recruitment reducing bargaining power, training lowering turnover) are clearly articulated and supported with relevant figures.  Yet several sectionsâparticularly the SWOT âOpportunitiesâ and the Maslow mappingâremain descriptive, and quantitative depth (ROI on training, turnover cost savings) is missing.  The analysis is clear and mostly supported, but not yet sophisticated.

Most quotations, figures and financial statistics are integrated to substantiate pointsâfor example, turnover data from Tajâs annual report underpin retention arguments, and BLS figures contextualise industry averages.  Some claims, however, rely on generic statements (e.g., âintense competitionâ or âemployees feel pressureâ) without direct evidence, and links between SWOT observations and cited data are occasionally loose.  Overall application is mostly relevant and appropriate but not consistently rigorous.

The student draws on annual reports, HBR case material, industry statistics, employee-review websites and academic textsâdemonstrating a broadly relevant source base.  Nevertheless, the methodology section is descriptive; it lacks explicit criteria for source credibility, triangulation or data-selection rationale.  Primary research is absent, and limitations (e.g., potential bias in online reviews) are acknowledged only implicitly later in the essay.  Hence the range is generally appropriate, but evidence of deliberate, informed selection is moderate rather than strong.

The essay draws on a wide spread of secondary evidence that is clearly pertinent to the question (annual reports, HBR case, labour statistics, brand-finance rankings).  Source relevance is usually explicit, and multiple perspectivesâfinancial, cultural and employeeâare triangulated in the evaluation section.  Nonetheless, primary data are absent and some sources (e.g., student blogs) are of uneven academic quality, so the research is solid rather than exemplary.

Basic layout conventions are followed (pagination, captioned figures, alphabetised bibliography), yet several inconsistencies remain: mixed citation styles, broken URL line breaks, irregular figure numbering and occasional typographical errors.  These lapses disrupt visual coherence, indicating that some formatting considerations have been missed or applied incorrectly.

The introduction provides a concise industry overview, a clear organisational focus (the Taj Group) and an explicit explanation of why HR-driven performance is worth investigating.  Key terms such as âtalent managementâ, âTajnessâ and âorganisational performanceâ are defined in context, allowing the reader to understand the parameters and significance of the study.  Throughout the essay the topic is revisitedâe.g., each analytical section explicitly links back to how recruitment, training and recognition shape outcomesâdemonstrating consistent, accurate and comprehensive communication of the topic.

All mandatory sections (title page, contents, intro, methodology, analysis, conclusion, bibliography) are present and logically ordered; headings and sub-headings create a coherent flow.  Minor issuesâover-long tables of contents dots, occasional cut-off sentencesâdetract slightly but do not compromise appropriateness.  Overall, the structure clearly meets the requirements.

annotations.comment: The inclusion of the investigation title at the very start meets the formatting requirement, providing clear orientation for the reader.
Repetitive citation notation ([HBR][Sapkala]) affects readability; consolidate or adopt a streamlined reference approach.
The leader dots in the table of contents are inconsistent in length, which disrupts visual alignment; streamline dot leaders for uniformity.
The introduction uses relevant industry statistics and reputable sources, demonstrating clear and appropriate application of source material.
Consider contrasting STARS/TPP with alternative recognition models to critically evaluate their relative effectiveness.
The table of contents lists all major sections in logical order, supporting clear navigation and appropriate report structure.
The research question is clearly stated but remains broad; consider specifying performance metrics or a time frame to sharpen focus and guide analysis.
The word count is provided immediately below the research question, satisfying the requirement to state total words at the beginning.
The rationale describes aims descriptively but lacks connection to specific performance metrics; deepen focus by linking scope to measurable outcomes.
The concept of âTajnessâ is well-defined using industry sources, showing clear understanding of organisational culture influencing HR.
Figure 2 presents turnover rates effectively; consider adding a comparative trend analysis over multiple years for deeper insight.
The phrase âensures a diverse and value driven workforceâ is duplicated; remove redundancy to enhance precision.
The sentence âEstablished in 1903 by Jamshedji Tata, is a prestigious hospitality chainâ is a fragment; revise to maintain grammatical coherence.
Citation style alternates between bracketed abbreviations and formal references; adopt a consistent style (e.g., Harvard or APA) throughout.
The methodology lists secondary sources but omits explicit criteria for source selection and credibility; include justification for chosen methods.
Theoretical frameworks from the syllabus are accurately identified and aligned with research objectives, demonstrating sound subject knowledge.
The link between culture and HR outcomes is asserted but could be strengthened with specific examples or employee performance data.
Describe more explicitly how Porterâs Five Forces will be used to evaluate HR practices, linking each force to talent management outcomes.
Providing stipend amounts and training duration reflects precise data application, reinforcing factual credibility.
Opportunities from expansion are noted; enhance by linking projected growth with specific talent management strategies needed.
Application of Herzberg and Maslow is largely descriptive; deepen analysis by linking each identified practice directly to theoretical constructs.
Figure 1 is clear but is not explicitly referred to in the surrounding analysis; integrate the diagram into discussion with precise theory citations.
Industry turnover data is used well; discuss potential data limitations or margins of error to demonstrate critical evaluation.
Long URLs break across lines awkwardly; apply line-breaking rules or use DOI/shortened links to maintain formatting integrity.
Accurate use of âintrinsic motivationâ shows consistent command of key motivational terminology.
A single employee review underscores workâlife balance issues but lacks representativeness; include aggregated review data or survey results.
The bibliography is extensive but exhibits mixed citation styles; select and apply a single style (e.g., APA or Harvard) consistently.
The SWOT section would benefit from quantitative evidence (e.g., retention rates or cost savings) to support identified strengths and threats.
Figure 3 caption placement interrupts text flow; reposition caption immediately below the image to improve readability.
The conclusion directly answers the research question but would be stronger by referencing key performance metrics discussed earlier.
The weakness regarding limited diversity is valid; bolster this point with demographic or recruitment data for depth of analysis.
The discussion of rising training costs cites precise figures but lacks analysis of ROI or costâbenefit implications.
Reference to âmoments of truthâ is insightful; define this concept operationally and discuss its impact on employee workload.
Porterâs Five Forces sub-headings use list formatting rather than formal headings; convert to standard heading styles for consistency.
Threat of new entrants is discussed in depth, but barriers such as capital investment requirements or regulatory hurdles are not addressed.
The link between buyer power and talent management is clear; strengthen by including customer satisfaction metrics to substantiate claims.
